exercises.title: Complete the class declaration
exercises.scenario: You're starting your first Java file. Fill in the missing parts to declare a class called Greeting.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to complete the class declaration. The class should be named Greeting and should be public.

blanks.hint: What modifier makes the class accessible from anywhere?
The class name should match what we asked for

hints: Java class declarations follow the pattern: <modifier> class <ClassName>
The class name in this exercise should be 'Greeting' with capital G
Don't forget 'public' as the modifier

exercises.title: Complete the main method signature
exercises.scenario: The main method is the entry point. Fill in the exact signature.
exercises.instructions: Complete the main method signature. Remember: the signature must be exact for the JVM to recognize it.

blanks.hint: Keyword that means 'belongs to the class, not an instance'
Return type when method returns nothing

hints: The signature is: public static void main(String[] args)
Each part has a specific meaning we'll cover later — for now, memorize it
The parameter type is String[] (capital S)

exercises.title: Add the missing semicolon
exercises.scenario: Java statements end with semicolons. Find the missing one.
exercises.instructions: This code has one missing semicolon that's preventing it from compiling. Add it to make the code work.

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Line one
Line two
Line three

hints: Look carefully at the end of each println statement
Compare line by line — what's different about line 2?
Line 'Line two' is missing its semicolon

exercises.title: Your first complete program
exercises.scenario: Write your very first Java program from scratch. This is the kind of thing you'll do hundreds of times in your career — getting it right the first time matters.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete Java program that prints exactly: Hello, Java!

The class should be called FirstProgram. Get the syntax right — no shortcuts.
exercises.starter_code: // Write your complete Java program below
hints: Start with the class declaration: public class FirstProgram
Inside the class, add the main method
Inside main, use System.out.println to print the message
Don't forget closing braces and semicolons

exercises.title: Multi-line output
exercises.scenario: You need to display a multi-line welcome message for a new app. Each line should appear on its own line.
exercises.instructions:
Write a program in a class called Welcome that prints these three lines:

Line 1: Welcome to CodeQuest
Line 2: Your coding journey starts here
Line 3: Let's build something great

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Welcome to CodeQuest
Your coding journey starts here
Let's build something great

hints: You'll need three separate println statements
Each println automatically adds a newline at the end
Make sure each statement ends with a semicolon

exercises.title: Print on same line
exercises.scenario: Sometimes you want output on the same line — like a progress indicator or inline status. Use print() instead of println().
exercises.instructions:
Write a class called SameLine that produces this exact output (note: 'Loading' and the dots are on the same line, followed by 'Done!' on a new line):

Loading...Done!
Application started
hints: Use print() for output that stays on the same line
Use println() when you want a newline at the end
'Loading', '...', and 'Done!' should be print/println combinations
Last line uses println() for the newline

exercises.title: Predict the output
exercises.scenario: Reading code accurately is as important as writing it. Predict exactly what this program prints.
hints: Note: print() does NOT add a newline, println() does
The first three lines build up one line of output
The fourth line creates a new line with just '!'

exercises.title: Fix the broken code
exercises.scenario: Your teammate sent you this code asking why it won't compile. Find ALL the bugs and fix them.
exercises.instructions: This code has 3 bugs preventing it from compiling. Find and fix all of them.

bugs.issue: Class name should be capitalized: BuggyCode (also file must match)
main method has wrong signature: Main (capital M) and String missing []
Missing semicolon at end of println statement

hints: Java is case-sensitive — 'main' is different from 'Main'
By convention, class names start with capital letter (PascalCase)
The main method signature must be exact, including String[] args
Don't forget semicolons at the end of statements

exercises.title: Format an introduction
exercises.scenario: You're writing a small program for an onboarding screen. The output needs specific formatting with separators.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class called Introduction that prints this exact output (the === lines are part of the output):

=====================
     INTRODUCTION
=====================
Name: Priya Sharma
Role: Backend Developer
Company: TechCorp
=====================

test_cases.expected_stdout:
=====================
     INTRODUCTION
=====================
Name: Priya Sharma
Role: Backend Developer
Company: TechCorp
=====================

hints: Pay attention to exact spacing — the title 'INTRODUCTION' has 5 spaces before it
The separator line has 21 equal signs (count carefully)
Use 7 separate println statements
Spaces matter in strings — copy carefully

exercises.title: Tricky output prediction
exercises.scenario: Predict what this slightly tricky code produces. Pay attention to print vs println.
hints: print() puts text directly with no newline
println() adds a newline AFTER the text
println() with no argument just prints a newline
Trace it carefully line by line

exercises.title: Find the subtle bugs
exercises.scenario: This code looks correct at first glance but has subtle issues. Real-world debugging often involves finding these.
exercises.instructions:
This code has 4 subtle bugs. Find and fix all of them so it compiles and prints:

Welcome to Java
Let's get started

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Welcome to Java
Let's get started

hints: Java is strictly case-sensitive
Keywords like 'class', 'void', 'public' are all lowercase
Standard library 'System.out' has specific capitalization
Compare line 3 and line 4 — they're inconsistent

exercises.title: Onboarding screen
exercises.scenario: You're building an onboarding screen for a new dev joining a startup. The output needs to feel professional and structured.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class called Onboarding that produces this exact output:

+-------------------------+
|   NEW HIRE ONBOARDING   |
+-------------------------+
  Step 1: Setup laptop
  Step 2: Install IDE
  Step 3: Clone repo
  Step 4: Run hello world
+-------------------------+
  Status: Ready to code!

test_cases.expected_stdout:
+-------------------------+
|   NEW HIRE ONBOARDING   |
+-------------------------+
  Step 1: Setup laptop
  Step 2: Install IDE
  Step 3: Clone repo
  Step 4: Run hello world
+-------------------------+
  Status: Ready to code!

hints: Count the dashes in the border — there are 25 in '-------------------------'
Steps are indented with 2 spaces
Title 'NEW HIRE ONBOARDING' has 3 spaces on each side
Use println() for each line

exercises.title: Refactor verbose code
exercises.scenario: Your teammate wrote this verbose code. It works but it's not great. Improve it while keeping exact same output.
exercises.instructions:
The code below works correctly but uses println for everything. Refactor to use a mix of print and println where appropriate, AND reduce the number of statements while keeping EXACT same output.

Original output:
Loading database...OK
Loading config...OK
Server started

Original code below:

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Loading database...OK
Loading config...OK
Server started

test_cases.description: Should use 3 println statements (plus class/method boilerplate)

hints: Strings can contain '...' directly — no need to print them separately
Each line of output should be ONE println statement
You can shorten 7 statements to 3

exercises.title: Complete program from spec
exercises.scenario: You receive a ticket from your project manager with these exact specifications. Build it.
exercises.instructions:
BUILD SPEC:
- Class name: ProductInfo
- Filename: ProductInfo.java
- Must compile without warnings
- Must produce this exact output:

========================================
             PRODUCT INFO
========================================
Product: Wireless Headphones
Brand: SoundMax
Price: $89.99
Stock: 47 units
----------------------------------------
Free shipping on orders over $50
Return policy: 30 days
========================================
hints: Border line has 40 equal signs
Separator line has 40 dashes
Title 'PRODUCT INFO' has 13 spaces before it (it's centered in 40 char width)
Watch out for the dollar sign — it's part of the string, no escaping needed

exercises.title: Build a structured report
exercises.scenario: You're writing the first program for a monitoring tool that prints daily server status. Output formatting matters in real tools because it's read by other engineers.
exercises.instructions:
Write a class called DailyReport that produces this output. Match it EXACTLY including all spaces and special characters:

╔══════════════════════════════════════╗
║         DAILY SERVER REPORT          ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════╝
  Server:      production-01
  Uptime:      99.97%
  Requests:    1,247,392
  Errors:      23
  Status:      [HEALTHY]

  Generated: 2026-05-18 09:00 UTC
hints: The box uses Unicode characters: ╔ ═ ╗ ║ ╚ ╝
Each data line has 2 spaces of indentation
Labels (Server:, Uptime:, etc.) are followed by spaces to align values
Use println() with no argument for the blank line
These special characters work fine in Java strings — no escaping needed
